macro_events.event_summary: Conflicting signals: Trump hints at positive Iran developments while Tehran claims the Strait of Hormuz is closed, creating oil supply disruption risk.
primary_drivers: Strait of Hormuz handles ~20% of global seaborne oil — closure spikes crude prices
Tanker rerouting and war-risk premiums benefit shippers
Higher energy input costs hurt airlines and transport-heavy consumer names

beneficiaries.rationale: Large integrated with substantial US/Permian upstream production — direct leverage to Brent/WTI spike from Hormuz closure, limited Iran/Gulf physical asset risk relative to peers.
US-weighted integrated oil major; benefits from crude price spike with Permian and Gulf of Mexico production. Tengiz Kazakhstan exposure is a minor offset but CPC pipeline is non-Hormuz.
Pure-play Permian E&P with no Middle East exposure — highest torque domestic name to a WTI spike driven by Hormuz closure.
Oilfield services/subsea equipment; higher crude prices drive capex cycle and near-term order flow expectations.
Product tanker operator — Hormuz disruption forces rerouting around Africa, ton-mile demand and spot rates spike materially, as seen in prior Red Sea/Hormuz scares.
Crude tanker operator; war-risk insurance surcharges and rerouting around closed Hormuz lanes drive VLCC/Suezmax rates sharply higher.
Cheniere — Qatari LNG exports transit Hormuz; closure removes ~20% of global LNG supply, making US Gulf LNG the marginal replacement with pricing power.
Defense prime with missile defense (THAAD, PAC-3) directly relevant to Gulf escalation; Patriot restocking and allied FMS demand accelerate on Iran confrontation.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25% of operating cost; crude spike from Hormuz closure compresses margins immediately, and Delta has less fuel hedging than peers historically.
International route exposure (Middle East/India overflight rerouting) plus jet fuel cost shock — double hit from Gulf escalation.
Domestic carrier with thin margins and historically unhedged fuel — most sensitive US airline to a WTI spike.
Bunker fuel costs spike with crude; Middle East itineraries (Dubai/Gulf) require rerouting. Consumer discretionary cyclicality adds risk in a geopolitical shock.
Fuel surcharge lag plus air freight rerouting around Gulf airspace — margin pressure from an oil shock of this magnitude.
Transportation/diesel cost passthrough and consumer spending pressure from higher gasoline prices; thin retail margins amplify the hit.

headlines: Trump says U.S. has 'good news' on Iran as Tehran says the Strait of Hormuz is closed again
